title: The poorly-controlled diabetic with new cataracts and numb feet
blurb: A sugar that gets trapped where it shouldn't — driving osmotic and oxidative damage in the lens, nerve, retina, and kidney of a chronically hyperglycemic patient.

patient.complaint: Blurry vision and 'pins and needles' in both feet over the past few months.

opening: 19-year-old with type 1 diabetes since age 8, admittedly poor adherence (frequent missed insulin, A1c 12.4%). Over a few months: progressive blurry vision and bilateral foot numbness/tingling. Slit-lamp shows early bilateral posterior subcapsular cataracts; monofilament testing shows reduced sensation in a stocking distribution. Fasting glucose 280. What do you want to ask, examine, or order next?
history: T1DM x 11 years, on basal-bolus insulin but frequently skips doses. No alcohol. No other meds. No family history of early cataracts. Reports nocturia and polydipsia (chronic hyperglycemia). No prior eye exam in 3 years.
exam: Vitals normal, BMI 22. Slit-lamp: bilateral posterior subcapsular lens opacities. Visual acuity 20/40 OU. Feet: warm, pulses intact, but reduced vibration and 10-g monofilament sensation to mid-shin bilaterally; ankle reflexes diminished. No ulcers. Fundus: scattered microaneurysms (early retinopathy).

prompts.diagnosis: What unifying biochemical mechanism links the cataracts, neuropathy, retinopathy, and early nephropathy?
prompts.cause: Which enzyme initiates this pathway, and what does it produce?
prompts.mechanism: Briefly: why do the lens and nerve accumulate so much of this product while other tissues are spared?

pinpoint.prompt: Click the enzyme that initiates the damaging pathway in chronic hyperglycemia.
pinpoint.correctMsg: Yes — aldose reductase. Under hyperglycemia it converts excess glucose to sorbitol (consuming NADPH). Tissues rich in aldose reductase but poor in sorbitol dehydrogenase (lens, retina, Schwann cells, kidney) trap sorbitol → osmotic swelling, plus NADPH depletion → less glutathione → oxidative injury.
pinpoint.wrongMsg: Not quite. The first, committed step converts glucose into the trapped sugar alcohol that drives osmotic damage.
pinpoint.revealMsg: Target: Aldose reductase (Glucose → Sorbitol). It's also the enzyme that makes galactitol from galactose in galactosemia — the same osmotic-cataract mechanism.

mcqs.stem: Why do the lens, Schwann cells, retina, and renal tissue accumulate sorbitol while, say, hepatocytes do not?
choices: These tissues express aldose reductase but have little sorbitol dehydrogenase, so sorbitol is made but not cleared — and being polar, it's trapped intracellularly
Sorbitol is actively imported into these tissues
mcqs.explanation: Aldose reductase makes sorbitol; sorbitol dehydrogenase converts it onward to fructose. Tissues high in the former and low in the latter trap sorbitol. Sorbitol is membrane-impermeant, so it accumulates osmotically — swelling the lens (cataract) and Schwann cells (neuropathy).

mcqs.stem: Beyond osmotic swelling, how does the polyol pathway add OXIDATIVE stress?
choices: Aldose reductase consumes NADPH, depleting the glutathione system and leaving cells vulnerable to reactive oxygen species
Sorbitol is itself a free radical
The pathway generates hydrogen peroxide directly
mcqs.explanation: Aldose reductase uses NADPH. Diverting NADPH starves glutathione reductase (which needs NADPH to regenerate reduced glutathione), lowering antioxidant capacity. So the polyol pathway is a double hit: osmotic (sorbitol trapping) plus oxidative (NADPH/glutathione depletion).

mcqs.stem: Which sugar uses this SAME aldose reductase step to cause infantile cataracts in a different inborn error?
choices: Galactose → galactitol in galactosemia / galactokinase deficiency
Fructose → fructitol in hereditary fructose intolerance
mcqs.explanation: Aldose reductase also reduces galactose to galactitol. In classic galactosemia and galactokinase deficiency, galactitol accumulates in the lens and causes infantile cataracts by the identical osmotic mechanism — the conceptual bridge to the dietary-sugars case.
